title: My story in so many wordssplit 20 SPOILERS not spoiler
display_title: My story in so many wordssplit 20 SPOILERS not spoiler
wikitext:
[]: My story, in so many words.(split 2.0 SPOILERS, not spoiler)             Quote Post by Tab » Sun Jul 05, 2009 4:43 pm Split by myself from here because it is way off the original topic and practically a damn dissertation. I'd like to interject for a moment about the whole "noob to the forum = noob to Eva" thing, with a bit less sass this time. Note: This post got huge . Like 1500 words huge. Five pages double-spaced in a word processor. Use it for a school essay if you like. If you take one thing away from it and read nothing else, let it be the final full paragraph (And to briefly ... if this were not true.) I joined today. I did not even know about this forum until a few weeks ago. My friend has mentioned evageeks.org in the past regarding Untitled Eva Project but I never actually looked at the site. The wiki has some good content. The forum is full of discussion about the new movies. The sequel theory is what drew me here to begin with those weeks ago. I came back today to grab some info from the wiki for a friend, and couldn't help but notice all the news about 2.0, so I hopped in. Literally joined the forum to comment on the Shinji's audio player bit that nobody picked up back on page 5. On the contrary I've been watching Eva since the ADV discs came out in 2000. Saw 0:1 in FYE, thought it was cool, and asked for it with a DVD player for Christmas that year. I suppose 2001 would be fairer to say that I began watching since I did get 0:1 for Christmas 2000. I caught up on what was released and tagged along until September when the last disc came out. Conveniently I was taking a trip to NYC in August, and I happened upon an anime import shop in Chinatown, picked up a pretty decent sub of D&R and EoE which of course hadn't been dubbed at that point. Was a good fanboy and didn't watch it until I was able to finish the series. Eva changed my life forever, in no small way. While I did not get 100% of the undertones at such a young age (14), what I did take away was far and away one of the major points. I used to say to people who complained about how Eva is so complicated to understand, that it is really very simple so long as you understand the concept of the AT Field. As far as I could perceive at that point this was the focal point – the concept of that barrier segregating our hearts and our minds from others, and which is the source of our suffering as individual entities. It gelled well with me at that point because I was falling in love for the first time, and experiencing the pain that comes with falling in love for the first time. In fact, I have documents from late 2001 using the concept of AT Field as a metaphor in regards to aspects of our level of closeness. And man that is nerdy as hell I am now realizing in retrospect. As I became older Eva continued to be an influence. I had been hooked in by its psychological and mystical subplots irrevocably. Any time I was feeling particularly down, I would sit down and give the then-unofficially-subbed EoE a watch, and it would cheer me up. I never understood why people said it was depressing. My translation had pretty well-interpreted quotes from Yui at the end saying, "Anywhere can be heaven, so long as you choose to live," (ADV has it as, "Anywhere can be paradise, so long as you have the will to live" if I recall; I actually greatly prefer the possibly poor phrasing of the unofficial sub) and "As long as the Sun, the Moon, and the Earth exist, it will be alright." They struck me at the time, I get choked up still now recalling them to transcribe for this post. Even Misato's, "If it's too painful, you can always run away." comforted me in a sense despite it being completely contrary to Anno's intentions for what the viewer should take away. In very real ways Eva helped me through many difficult phases of growing up. Perhaps exceptionally so due to how well I related to Gendo at that point, resonating with his pain regarding Yui, and his sole drive to be reunited with her at the cost of the very world as we know it. This dialogue is still one of my favorites out of any dialogue in any medium: Gendo, Kaoru, Yui, and Rei respectively said not wrote: "I didn't believe that anyone could love me. I never deserved to be loved." "So, you were running away. And you rejected the others around you, so there was no chance you would ever be hurt." "And you were terrified by the invisible bonds that people form." "You were afraid, and so, you closed off your heart."

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sun Jul 05, 2009 4:52 pm Excellent post, and welcome to the forums! I don't have too much to add, but I do like what you said. He wants everyone to learn the easy way what he learned by suffering five long years of severe depression, and this is very important: That your life is meaningful, and that nobody dictates what that meaning is but you; so stop worrying and enjoy it, experience it, and never flee from your heart.

[]:             Quote Post by Barong » Sun Jul 05, 2009 4:58 pm This is indeed a very insightful and beautiful post, I see much resemblance to my own endeavor with NGE in it. Thinking about how many people on here share these kind of feelings towards the series it's only all the more a mystery to me how they can be so hostile, yeah ... downright evil to one another. We of all people should stick together and encourage each other, me iz zinking. Anyways:

[]:             Quote Post by Tab » Sun Jul 05, 2009 4:59 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: I've been struggling on and off with moderate/severe depression since my teenage years (I'm 23 and female), and this is an absolutely beautiful statement (it brought tears to my eyes). I agree completely.

[]: Re: My story, in so many words.(split 2.0 SPOILERS, not spoi             Quote Post by schismatics » Sun Jul 05, 2009 5:46 pm Tab wrote: But Evangelion is one among two anime which I will not ever deny.

[]: Re: My story, in so many words.(split 2.0 SPOILERS, not spoi             Quote Post by Tab » Sun Jul 05, 2009 5:53 pm schismatics wrote: THIS.

[]:             Quote Post by Oshima » Sun Jul 05, 2009 5:54 pm Well worth the 1500 words (or more). welcome to the forums. It seems we all have more in common then it would first appear...then again that's not very surprising is it? i'm happy to have read your post, thanks for sharing :) I'm curious to know what the other anime you won't give up on is. EDIT: seems i took to long replying and it's been answered..heheh

[]:             Quote Post by Zaque » Sun Jul 05, 2009 5:58 pm @Tab: Thanks for that post, I don't know too much about the different philosophies, but after looking up solipsism and reading what you wrote on how Anno is different in that respect, I can certainly see the connection between his way of thinking and how souls are handled in Evangelion. Although, I wonder if there's a different term besides solipsism that would fit better, as it would seem that the existence of the other is a major part of eva, and the AT Fields attest to that. From what I've gathered, this other existence is contrary to the entire notion of solipsism, which is that only one mind exists. Which would make the idea that there are "different" minds perceiving unique realities somewhat unnecessary as there would only be that one mind to begin with. Anyway, I was hoping you could either find something more suitable, or tell me if this "branch" off of solipsism really is the closest we can get (in which case, I would like to think of it is a different philosophy altogether).

[]:             Quote Post by Tab » Sun Jul 05, 2009 6:11 pm Zaque wrote: @Tab: Thanks for that post, I don't know too much about the different philosophies, but after looking up solipsism and reading what you wrote on how Anno is different in that respect, I can certainly see the connection between his way of thinking and how souls are handled in Evangelion. Although, I wonder if there's a different term besides solipsism that would fit better, as it would seem that the existence of the other is a major part of eva, and the AT Fields attest to that. From what I've gathered, this other existence is contrary to the entire notion of solipsism, which is that only one mind exists. Which would make the idea that there are "different" minds perceiving unique realities somewhat unnecessary as there would only be that one mind to begin with. Anyway, I was hoping you could either find something more suitable, or tell me if this "branch" off of solipsism really is the closest we can get (in which case, I would like to think of it is a different philosophy altogether).

[]:             Quote Post by Zaque » Sun Jul 05, 2009 6:59 pm Ok, let's just say I got caught up in the first line of the Wikipedia entry then... I mean, I get what you're saying, but it seems contradictory to what was presented in the series. Furthermore, I checked out one of the external links from wikipedia, and it more or less solidified my thought that "anno's solipsism" isn't really solipsism, although I will admit that the interactions with others are handled somewhat similarly. I'll check out the youtube videos sometime later, but for the time being, I think some of the major points in anno's philosophies may captured better in something other than solipsism. I would say something regarding that those thoughts occurred during instrumentality when all souls were "merging" rather than in the real world, but I'm not exactly sure how to phrase it. To me, it seems as though evangelion is more rooted in the physical world even though much of what is important internally seems to reject it at times. hmm... i guess what i'm trying to get at is that although the way the characters perceive the world may be somewhat solipsistic in nature, that the outside world and others actually exist and are real, defeats the purpose of what solipsism actually is, which as you said, was skeptical hypothesis.

[]:             Quote Post by schismatics » Sun Jul 05, 2009 7:20 pm

[]:             Quote Post by Tab » Sun Jul 05, 2009 7:28 pm Zaque wrote: Ok, let's just say I got caught up in the first line of the Wikipedia entry

[]:             Quote Post by MugwumpHasNoLiver » Mon Jul 06, 2009 12:21 am Well done, everyone in this topic. You've all managed to accurately describe MY introduction and feelings towards this series as well. So are there any Eva geeks who were NOT exposed to this show as a depressed adolescent and found it a life-changing experience?

[]:             Quote Post by Tab » Mon Jul 06, 2009 12:29 am MugwumpHasNoLiver wrote: Well done, everyone in this topic. You've all managed to accurate describe MY introduction and feelings towards this series as well. So are there any Eva geeks who were NOT exposed to this show as a depressed adolescent and found it a life-changing experience?

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Jul 06, 2009 1:58 am MugwumpHasNoLiver wrote: So are there any Eva geeks who were NOT exposed to this show as a depressed adolescent and found it a life-changing experience?

[]:             Quote Post by Zaque » Mon Jul 06, 2009 2:33 am Ok, so I watched the series on "Anno's solipsism" on youtube, and from what I could tell, there was a heavy emphasis on the importance of the other in order to know yourself, that others perception of you are almost at the same importance as your own perception. It talked about ones ability to change one's perception of oneself, and to live in the reality that exists. And about reality itself, I felt the statement emphasized more that one has the power to change one's perception of that reality rather than the reality is merely a construct of ones own mind (yes, I know those two are practically the same, but the main difference is that in the former, it assumes that the reality outside of us is "real" so to speak and it is up to us to take it how we feel like, whereas the latter is more along the lines that the reality outside of us isn't really outside of us but rather only what we think of it. hopefully i explained that rather than just repeating myself...). It's subtle difference, but an important one. Also, most of the parts where I see strong solipsism being channeled were when the characters were talking about Shinji's choice for instrumentality, a world where he is the only one in "control" so to speak, rather than his eventual decision to break away from it and return to a world where he exists along with others, only then being able to change his perceptions of how he feels about both himself and the others to perhaps "live better", or at least less isolated/cowardly/self-depreciating/etc. That said, the stuff about one's perception certainly has solipsistic aspects to it, such as the part of all one knows is what one has perceived, but I think it's more of a statement on perception itself rather than touting a full solipsistic philosophy. It doesn't "reject" the world other than your mind as just a construct of your mind (again, the part about rejecting the outside world is associated with the instrumentality Shinji wished for), but rather accepts it and deals with it by altering ones perceptions of it (associated with Shinji accepting himself and the world for who he is/what it is, and taking a step to live the "outside world", outside his mind). there really is two worlds then, the one outside including the existence of others, and the private one only to individuals which deals with identity (see AT Fields again) and how one feels about others and others perception of the self. Regarding the wikipedia entry again, and those excepts you've posted, they all really point to the same thing that all outside truths cannot be known completely, again, with the skepticism. What i said above was that it wasn't really this thought that was trying to be broadcast by the series, but rather one determines his own truth (albeit through perception). Yes, others can not be "known", but the point of the matter is that these others exist, something that resembles more of a materialistic view of the world than an idealistic, with solipsism being idealistic ( also on that page ). About the relation of solipsism and eastern philosophy, mmm...., well, I can't really say that the show did or did not have aspects of Zen Buddhism in this regard as Zen Buddhism is pretty much compatible with what was said, although perhaps a layer removed, so maybe an application of it, I'm not sure. That there were no "direct" statements on the oneness of the self and the universe leads me to believe that that wasn't what was intended though. But if you'll note, it says these things were similar to solipsism, but fundamentally, i believe that they are different as well. MugwumpHasNoLiver wrote: So are there any Eva geeks who were NOT exposed to this show as a depressed adolescent and found it a life-changing experience?

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Jul 06, 2009 6:25 am Zaque wrote: Interesting article. Not sure what you meant by "blinked" though...

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon Jul 06, 2009 5:04 pm Welcome to the board, Tab. You've made an excellent introduction. I've advocated this line of thought as well, though I think it might be better described as a universal state of double-blind idealism. How anyone can watch the original ending and not get a soplipsistic vibe though I'll never know. With regards to the larger human story I have to agree with Mr. Tines that EoE was a downer. I also submit that Anno was only really interested in investigating the personal rather than the metaphysical concepts in play, so taken on his own terms he succeed wildly regardless of whether one agrees with the message.

[]:             Quote Post by VoidEater » Mon Jul 06, 2009 5:53 pm "Anno Solipsism" is solipsism from multiple perspectives. It may be a form of existentialism. While Instrumentality may be portrayed as escapism, EoE does seem to make the point that this can only be learned from the perspective of having experienced it and evaluated it. Instrumentality may be solipsism ad absurdum. Excellent posts.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Jul 07, 2009 1:32 am This is an excellent post and while I don't have much to add to the "Anno's solipsism" discussion (I've always found the entire philosophy quite dubious, if not more than a bit sophistic), I just thought I'd post a link to my own personal NGE story if you're interested. I laugh at your petty 1500 words.

Article: My story in so many wordssplit 20 SPOILERS not spoiler
 What i said above was that it wasn't really this thought that was trying to be broadcast by the series, but rather one determines his own truth (albeit through perception). Yes, others can not be "known", but the point of the matter is that these others exist, something that resembles more of a materialistic view of the world than an idealistic, with solipsism being idealistic ( also on that page ). About the relation of solipsism and eastern philosophy, mmm...., well, I can't really say that the show did or did not have aspects of Zen Buddhism in this regard as Zen Buddhism is pretty much compatible with what was said, although perhaps a layer removed, so maybe an application of it, I'm not sure. That there were no "direct" statements on the oneness of the self and the universe leads me to believe that that wasn't what was intended though. But if you'll note, it says these things were similar to solipsism, but fundamentally, i believe that they are different as well. MugwumpHasNoLiver wrote: So are there any Eva geeks who were NOT exposed to this show as a depressed adolescent and found it a life-changing experience?

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon Jul 06, 2009 5:04 pm Welcome to the board, Tab. You've made an excellent introduction. I've advocated this line of thought as well, though I think it might be better described as a universal state of double-blind idealism. How anyone can watch the original ending and not get a soplipsistic vibe though I'll never know. With regards to the larger human story I have to agree with Mr. Tines that EoE was a downer. I also submit that Anno was only really interested in investigating the personal rather than the metaphysical concepts in play, so taken on his own terms he succeed wildly regardless of whether one agrees with the message.

Article: My story in so many wordssplit 20 SPOILERS not spoiler
 Jul 06, 2009 5:53 pm "Anno Solipsism" is solipsism from multiple perspectives. It may be a form of existentialism. While Instrumentality may be portrayed as escapism, EoE does seem to make the point that this can only be learned from the perspective of having experienced it and evaluated it. Instrumentality may be solipsism ad absurdum. Excellent posts.
